results.answer:
Root cause: process_refund uses the card_last4 field for UPI refunds instead of the Payment.vpa field, so UPI refunds send None (no card_last4) instead of the VPA [refund.py][payment.py][test_refund.py][stack_trace.log].

Exact code change (replace one line in refund.py):

*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: refund.py
@@
-    if payment.method == 'upi':
-        return issue_refund(payment.card_last4, payment.amount)
+    if payment.method == 'upi':
+        return issue_refund(payment.vpa, payment.amount)
*** End Patch

Resulting function:

def process_refund(payment):
    # UPI refunds must use the vpa, not card_last4
    if payment.method == 'upi':
        return issue_refund(payment.vpa, payment.amount)
    return issue_refund(payment.account_id, payment.amount)

This fixes the failing test which expected the UPI destination 'alice@bank' [test_refund.py].

Root cause
- process_refund uses the card_last4 field for UPI refunds instead of the VPA (vpa) field, so UPI refunds are sent to the wrong destination [refund.py]. The failing test expects the VPA 'alice@bank' for a UPI payment [test_refund.py], and the stack trace points to the return line using card_last4 as the cause of the failure [stack_trace.log][refund.py].

Exact code change
- In refund.py, inside process_refund, replace:
  return issue_refund(payment.card_last4, payment.amount)
  (original line) [refund.py]
- With:
  return issue_refund(payment.vpa, payment.amount)
  (use the VPA for UPI refunds — matches the test expectation) [test_refund.py]

Why this fixes it
- The test constructs a UPI Payment with vpa='alice@bank' and expects the refund destination to be that VPA [test_refund.py]; switching to payment.vpa makes the function return the expected destination instead of None (the current behavior shown in the stack trace) [stack_trace.log].
No — production deployments require release manager approval [deploy_policy_2026] (see Approval Matrix: production deploy requires release manager approval) [approval_matrix].

No. Deployments to production require approval from a release manager [deploy_policy_2026 §5.2]. Approval matrix: production deploys require release manager approval; production hotfixes require on‑call lead approval [approval_matrix §A.1]. Emergency deploys require post‑hoc approval within 24 hours [deploy_policy_2026 §5.2].

The current policy (Refund Policy v2.0) allows customers to request a refund within 30 days of purchase; refunds are issued to the original payment method, digital goods are refundable only if unused, and refunds are started by contacting support with your order number [refund_policy_2026].

- Section heading: "Refund Policy" — Current policy: Customers may request a refund within 30 days of purchase; refunds are issued to the original payment method and to start a refund customers must contact support with their order number [refund_policy_2026 §2.1].

- Section heading: "How long does a refund take under the refund policy?" — Processing time: a refund is processed within 5 to 7 business days after approval [faq_refunds §1.3].
